title: Join the Lab
text:
TipPostdoctoral Scholar — Quantitative Flux Analyses in the Human Gut



We are looking for a postdoctoral scholar with a strong background in physics, mathematics, or a related quantitative discipline to lead projects on flux analyses in the human gut microbiota. The position is centered on mathematical modeling to understand how gut bacteria metabolize substrates and produce fermentation products. Depending on interest, the position can also involve experimental work combining HPLC, bioreactors, and anaerobic culturing techniques.
Please contact Jonas with a CV and a brief description of your research interests.








TipPostdoctoral Scholar — Principles of Bacterial Growth Physiology and Metabolism



We are looking for a postdoctoral scholar to work on the physiological principles underlying bacterial growth, with a focus on the interdependence of metabolism, growth, and cellular composition — including envelope and proteome allocation. The position emphasizes quantitative experimentation across a range of conditions (growth physiology, proteomics, microscopy) tightly integrated with mathematical modeling. Experience in microbiology, biophysics, or quantitative biology is welcome.
Please contact Jonas with a CV and a brief description of your research interests.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Postdocs
We are always interested in hearing from exceptional postdoctoral candidates. In addition to the open positions above, feel free to reach out if our research directions resonate with your interests — we are happy to discuss potential projects and funding options.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Graduate Students
Not yet at Stanford? Please feel free to reach out to Jonas — we are always happy to connect with prospective students. Consider applying to the Biology graduate program, the Biophysics program, or any other Bio Sciences graduate program at Stanford.
If you are currently enrolled at Stanford and interested in doing a rotation in our lab, please contact Jonas to schedule a meeting. We love to hear about your scientific ideas and research interests.
We have experimental projects available in microbiology and microbiota research — from fundamental questions of bacterial physiology to more biomedical questions. Experimentally, we focus on the quantitative culturing of bacteria, integrating bioreactors, microscopy, HPLC, and mathematical modeling.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Undergraduate Students
We participate in the Bio-X undergraduate summer research program and BSURP. Feel free to contact Jonas with information about your background and interests.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Lab Community
To learn more about our commitment to building an inclusive and collaborative lab environment, please read our lab values and community guidelines.

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Laboratory of Quantitative Microbial Physiology
  
    We combine experimentation, data analysis, and mathematical modeling to
    understand how microbial cells grow, adapt, and shape their environments —
    from individual E. coli to the human gut microbiota.
    Jonas Cremer lab · Stanford University · Department of Biology
  
  
    Our Research
    Meet the Team
    Publications

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Recent News

2025-06 PRX Life paper on diurnal variations in gut microbial dynamics featured in a Physics Viewpoint by Anne-Florence Bitbol. Read viewpoint →

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Research Themes
Microbial cell physiology How do cells coordinate growth across thousands of processes? We study the essential principles of microbial physiology in E. coli.
Physiology–ecology–evolution How do cell-physiological constraints shape adaptation and evolution in complex environments?
Human gut microbiota How do microbial physiology and intestinal conditions jointly determine gut microbiome composition and function?
→ Learn more about our research

title: Lab Community
text: We are committed to building a lab that is welcoming to all, regardless of ethnic, socioeconomic, gender, or sexual orientation background. We acknowledge the systemic failures that exist in academia and call on each lab member to actively help combat microaggressions and bias in our day-to-day work. Reports of harassment can be directed to the biology department’s reporting channels, and Jonas will always be available to discuss concerns directly.

title: Lab Community
text: We are committed to building a lab that is welcoming to all, regardless of ethnic, socioeconomic, gender, or sexual orientation background. We acknowledge the systemic failures that exist in academia and call on each lab member to actively help combat microaggressions and bias in our day-to-day work. Reports of harassment can be directed to the biology department’s reporting channels, and Jonas will always be available to discuss concerns directly.

title: Lab Community
section: We keep life and work in a healthy balance
text:
We keep life and work in a healthy balance
The health and wellbeing of lab members is our top priority. There is no expectation of evening or weekend work, nor of prompt replies to Slack or email outside of working hours. If you ever feel overwhelmed — by workload, personal circumstances, or anything else — please speak with Jonas. We want everyone to thrive both inside and outside the lab.

title: Lab Community
section: We respect the intellectual work of other lab members
text:
We respect the intellectual work of other lab members
Open exchange of ideas is one of the great joys of science. Collaborative projects require active, ongoing discussion around contributions, authorship, and attribution. We make these conversations explicit and early rather than leaving them for later.

title: Lab Community
section: We discuss openly but always respect our co-scientists
text:
We discuss openly but always respect our co-scientists
Ideas should be freely raised and challenged on scientific grounds. We maintain a culture of intellectual honesty and directness while always treating colleagues with respect, regardless of experience level or place in the lab hierarchy.

title: Lab Community
text:
Safety first
The lab operates at BSL-2. All safety procedures must be followed at all times. Communicate any doubts or concerns about safety practices to Jonas or the lab safety officer — there is no such thing as a trivial safety question.

title: Lab Community
section: We jointly contribute to keeping the lab well-maintained
text:
We jointly contribute to keeping the lab well-maintained
Everyone shares responsibility for chemicals, consumables, equipment, and waste. Participation in at least one annual lab cleaning day is expected from all members.

title: Lab Community
section: We respect each other’s experimental workspace
text:
We respect each other’s experimental workspace
A strict rule: personal bench space, chemical stocks, and pipettes must not be used without prior permission from the owner. When in doubt, ask.

title: Lab Community
text:
We promote open science
Manuscripts and data should be made openly available wherever possible — via preprint servers, GitHub, and data repositories. Raw data storage approaches are documented on the lab Wiki. Openness and reproducibility are values, not afterthoughts.

title: Research
text: Admittedly, we are a bit obsessed with microbial growth. How do tiny microbial cells harbor thousands of enzymes and coordinate a myriad of processes to replicate and thrive? Can we find in a sea of complexity the essential principles which determine growth? We approach these fundamental questions of systems biology by tightly combining experiments across conditions with mathematical modeling and data analysis — mostly for Escherichia coli, this wonderfully studied model organism, but increasingly also for other microbes across biological domains.

title: Research
text: Admittedly, we are a bit obsessed with microbial growth. How do tiny microbial cells harbor thousands of enzymes and coordinate a myriad of processes to replicate and thrive? Can we find in a sea of complexity the essential principles which determine growth? We approach these fundamental questions of systems biology by tightly combining experiments across conditions with mathematical modeling and data analysis — mostly for Escherichia coli, this wonderfully studied model organism, but increasingly also for other microbes across biological domains.

title: Research
section: Microbial life at the triangle of physiology, ecology, and evolution
text:
Microbial life at the triangle of physiology, ecology, and evolution
Our quantitative investigations also go well beyond the cellular level. Integrating ecological and evolutionary considerations, we explore how cell physiological constraints determine adaptation to specific environments and habitats.



The triangle of microbial life

title: Research
text:
The human gut microbiota
We further build on our cell-physiological understanding to better rationalize the composition of the human gut microbiota and its effect on host health. Towards a more mechanistic understanding, we follow quantitative approaches that combine in-vitro experimentation and mathematical modeling to link considerations of cell metabolism, microbial population dynamics, and intestinal physiology.

title: Courses
text: Current and previous teaching activities can also be found on Stanford ExploreCourses.

title: Courses
section: BIO 165/265 — Quantitative Approaches in Modern Biology
text:
BIO 165/265 — Quantitative Approaches in Modern Biology
Winter Quarter · Undergraduate (BIO 165) and Graduate (BIO 265)
Modern research approaches tightly integrate experimentation with data analysis and mathematical modeling to provide unprecedented insights into the organization and functioning of living systems. This course explores the quantitative basis of major cellular processes and their coordination to form a cohesive physiological entity capable of rapid growth and acclimation to changing environments. Weekly lectures are accompanied by dry-lab sessions in which students analyze experimental data sets and develop a fundamental skill set in quantitative biology — coding, dynamical systems modeling, and statistics.
→ ExploreCourses

title: Courses
text:
BIO 120/220 — Integrative and Experimental Microbiology
Spring Quarter · Undergraduate (BIO 120) and Graduate (BIO 220)
To survive, grow, and reproduce, organisms coordinate different molecular processes to use available resources and cope with environmental conditions. This laboratory course explores molecular and cellular integration in bacteria. Experiments include the quantification of growth, mutational screens and mutant analysis, gene cloning, and measuring/engineering gene expression. These wet-lab approaches are combined with dry-lab approaches to analyze experimental findings and explore the link between molecular processes, cell physiology, and ecology.
→ ExploreCourses
